title: Sorting kubectl Output
description:
## Sorting `kubectl` Output

The `--sort-by` flag on `kubectl get` allows you to sort output by any JSONPath expression — extremely useful for finding the newest pod, the heaviest resource consumer, or the oldest event.

**Your task:**

Several pods have been created for you. Complete the following:

1. List all pods in the `default` namespace **sorted by their creation timestamp** (oldest first) and save the output to `/tmp/pods-sorted.txt`
2. List all pods sorted by name and save to `/tmp/pods-by-name.txt`

```bash
# Sort by creation time:
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Sort by name:
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.name
```

hints.title: --sort-by flag syntax
hints.body: Pass any JSONPath expression to --sort-by. The expression must point to a comparable field (string, number, or timestamp).

hints.title: Save output to a file
hints.body: Redirect kubectl output using the > operator.
hints.command: kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp > /tmp/pods-sorted.txt && kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.name > /tmp/pods-by-name.txt

commands.description: File /tmp/pods-sorted.txt exists and contains pod output
File /tmp/pods-by-name.txt exists and contains pod output
pods-by-name.txt is sorted alphabetically (sort-pod-a appears before sort-pod-c)

teardown_commands.command: kubectl delete pod sort-pod-a sort-pod-b sort-pod-c --ignore-not-found --grace-period=0 --force
